heroOverview: Achlorhydria is the complete absence of hydrochloric acid secretion by the stomach's parietal cells, producing a gastric pH persistently above 7 rather than the normal fasting pH of 1.5–3.5. The stomach produces acid partly to sterilise ingested food, activate protein-digesting enzymes, and absorb key micronutrients; without it, the risk of small intestinal bacterial overgrowth, vitamin B12 deficiency, and iron-deficiency anaemia rises substantially. Long-term use of proton pump inhibitors is now the most common reversible cause worldwide, while autoimmune atrophic gastritis — in which the immune system destroys parietal cells — remains the classic irreversible form and carries a modestly elevated risk of gastric neoplasia. True achlorhydria is uncommon in the general population, estimated at under 1%, but subclinical hypochlorhydria (acid insufficiency) is far more prevalent, particularly in older adults.

keyStats.prevalence: True achlorhydria (gastric pH ≥7 on stimulation testing) estimated at less than 1% of adults; hypochlorhydria affects up to 30% of adults over age 65 (Krasinski et al.)
keyStats.demographics: Autoimmune form predominantly affects women aged 40-70 and those with other autoimmune conditions; PPI-induced achlorhydria affects users of all ages
keyStats.avgAge: Autoimmune atrophic gastritis typically presents between ages 40 and 70; drug-induced form can occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence varies by region; H. pylori infection, a leading cause of atrophic gastritis, affects approximately 44% of the global population

definition: Achlorhydria (ICD-10: K31.83) denotes the complete failure of gastric acid secretion, confirmed when intragastric pH fails to fall below 7.0 after maximal stimulation with pentagastrin or histamine. The condition sits at one end of a spectrum that includes hypochlorhydria (reduced acid output without complete absence) and is distinguished from anacidity by its definitional threshold. Parietal cells in the gastric body and fundus secrete HCl via a proton pump (H⁺/K⁺-ATPase); their destruction — by autoimmune attack, atrophy from chronic H. pylori infection, or long-term pharmacological suppression — produces achlorhydria. Gastric acid serves multiple functions: it converts pepsinogen to active pepsin for protein digestion; it releases vitamin B12 from food proteins so intrinsic factor can bind it; it converts ferric to ferrous iron facilitating absorption; and it constitutes the primary barrier against ingested microorganisms. Loss of these functions explains the characteristic nutritional deficiencies and infection susceptibility that define the clinical impact of the condition. Autoimmune atrophic gastritis (type A) targets parietal cells via anti-parietal-cell and anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies, simultaneously destroying the intrinsic factor needed for B12 absorption and thereby producing pernicious anaemia as a downstream consequence. Environmental atrophic gastritis (type B), driven by chronic H. pylori infection, causes progressive acid-gland loss and may recover partially after H. pylori eradication. PPI-induced profound acid suppression mimics achlorhydria biochemically during treatment but is fully reversible on drug cessation.

typesClassification.description: Caused by immune-mediated destruction of parietal cells; produces true achlorhydria alongside intrinsic-factor deficiency and pernicious anaemia. Associated with other autoimmune conditions including Hashimoto thyroiditis and type 1 diabetes. Carries a 1-3% lifetime risk of gastric neoplasia, warranting endoscopic surveillance.

H. pylori colonises the antrum and body of the stomach over years, inducing chronic inflammation that progressively destroys acid-secreting glands. Eradication can halt progression; some glandular recovery occurs in younger patients if treated before extensive atrophy develops.

Long-term high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy suppresses acid secretion profoundly, producing a state that meets biochemical criteria for achlorhydria while the drug is taken. This is fully reversible on stopping the PPI. H2 receptor antagonists cause lesser and reversible acid suppression.
Gastrectomy or vagotomy removes or denervates acid-secreting mucosa, producing permanent achlorhydria; affected patients require lifelong B12 and iron monitoring.
VIPoma and Zollinger-Ellison syndrome paradoxically appear related but produce opposite effects; achlorhydria can also arise from sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, or extensive gastric irradiation.

primarySymptoms: Bloating and early satiety after meals, caused by impaired protein digestion and bacterial fermentation of nutrients that would normally be processed in an acidic environment.
Nausea, often mild and chronic rather than acute, related to poor nutrient digestion and altered gastric emptying dynamics.
Diarrhoea or loose stools from bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine, which can ferment carbohydrates and produce gas, bloating, and watery stools.
Fatigue and pallor from iron-deficiency anaemia, since gastric acid normally converts dietary ferric iron to the absorbable ferrous form.
Neurological symptoms from vitamin B12 deficiency — numbness or tingling in the hands and feet, unsteady gait, and cognitive slowing — which can appear before anaemia in some patients.
Halitosis and a sense of gastric discomfort arising from fermentation of undigested food by colonising bacteria.
Unintentional weight loss in severe or long-standing cases where protein malabsorption reduces overall nutritional status.

Recurrent gastric and small intestinal bacterial infections, including Salmonella, Campylobacter, and Clostridioides difficile, because the loss of the acid bactericidal barrier lowers the infectious dose required to cause illness.
Brittle or ridged nails and hair loss as secondary signs of chronic iron or protein deficiency in patients with prolonged untreated achlorhydria.
Increased frequency of skin infections and oral thrush, reflecting broader immune compromise from micronutrient depletion.
earlyWarningSigns: Feeling bloated or gassy regularly after meals even without dietary indiscretion.
Gradually worsening fatigue that does not resolve with rest, prompting a blood count that shows iron-deficiency anaemia.
A tingling or pins-and-needles sensation in the feet or fingertips before any other obvious cause is found.
Recurrent gastrointestinal infections with organisms that do not normally cause disease in people with normal stomach acid.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe weakness, rapid heartbeat, and shortness of breath from profound anaemia, requiring urgent blood count and possible transfusion.

Acute neurological deterioration including ataxia, confusion, or vision changes from severe B12 deficiency, which is a medical emergency — untreated subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord can become irreversible.
Bloody vomiting or black tarry stools, which in the context of known atrophic gastritis may indicate a complicating gastric ulcer or neoplasm.
High fever with profuse watery diarrhoea, suggesting a serious enteric infection facilitated by loss of the acid barrier.

causes.description: In autoimmune atrophic gastritis, cytotoxic T lymphocytes and antibodies directed against the H⁺/K⁺-ATPase proton pump and against intrinsic factor progressively destroy parietal cells in the gastric body and fundus. The trigger for this autoimmune response is unknown in most patients, but it shares genetic susceptibility loci with other organ-specific autoimmune diseases.

causes.cause: Chronic H. pylori infection with atrophic progression
causes.description: H. pylori triggers a sustained inflammatory response that begins with non-atrophic gastritis and, over decades, in 10-15% of infected individuals, progresses to multifocal atrophic gastritis with gland loss. The bacteria colonise the antrum first, spreading to the body and destroying parietal cell mass over years. Eradication can halt progression if performed before extensive atrophy has occurred.

PPIs irreversibly block the H⁺/K⁺-ATPase for the lifetime of the enzyme; with continuous daily dosing, fasting intragastric pH can rise to 5–7, approaching achlorhydric levels. Reactive hypergastrinaemia drives parietal cell hyperplasia during use, and gastrin normalises on drug cessation, making this form completely reversible.

Total or near-total gastrectomy eliminates acid-secreting mucosa permanently. Vagotomy removes the neural stimulus to acid secretion without removing the parietal cells, producing sustained hypochlorhydria; some recovery of acid output occurs over years after truncal vagotomy.

Therapeutic radiation to the upper abdomen for gastric or oesophageal cancer can permanently damage the gastric mucosa, eliminating parietal cell function in the field. The severity of acid loss is dose-dependent and develops progressively over months after treatment ends.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: Autoimmune atrophic gastritis clusters with Hashimoto thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, Addison disease, and vitiligo; first-degree relatives of affected patients have substantially higher rates of parietal-cell antibodies.
Chronic H. pylori infection is the leading modifiable risk factor for environmental atrophic gastritis; eradication eliminates the inflammatory stimulus and reduces risk of further atrophy.
Gastric mucosal atrophy is common with ageing regardless of H. pylori status; gastric acid output declines by approximately 25% per decade from age 50 in population studies.
Autoimmune atrophic gastritis affects women approximately twice as often as men, consistent with the broader female predominance in organ-specific autoimmune disease.
Patients taking daily PPIs for more than five years show significant gastric acid suppression; reviewing the indication for ongoing PPI use with a clinician reduces unnecessary exposure.
Pre-existing nutritional deficiencies worsen the clinical impact of achlorhydria; adequate dietary intake reduces the burden on a compromised absorptive system.
Autoimmune atrophic gastritis (pernicious anaemia) has a markedly higher prevalence in Northern European populations compared with other groups, reaching 1-2% in some Scandinavian cohorts.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 40-70 with autoimmune atrophic gastritis
Adults over 60 with age-related mucosal atrophy
Long-term users of proton pump inhibitors
People with a personal or family history of organ-specific autoimmune disease
Individuals from high H. pylori prevalence regions, particularly Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and South America
Patients who have undergone gastrectomy or gastric irradiation
diagnosisOverview: Suspicion for achlorhydria typically arises when blood tests reveal iron-deficiency anaemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, or an unexpectedly elevated fasting serum gastrin — a compensatory response to absent acid. Serum testing for anti-parietal-cell antibodies (present in 90% of autoimmune atrophic gastritis) and anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies (more specific at 60-70%) is the first diagnostic step. Endoscopy with biopsies from the gastric body and antrum is the definitive investigation, demonstrating the distribution and severity of atrophy and intestinal metaplasia according to the OLGA or OLGIM staging systems. Intragastric pH measurement, formerly the gold standard, is now rarely performed as a standalone test; direct measurement with a wireless pH capsule or nasogastric probe can confirm achlorhydria when the diagnosis is otherwise ambiguous. Testing for active H. pylori infection (urea breath test, stool antigen, or biopsy urease test) is essential because eradication is the only modifiable intervention in environmental atrophic gastritis. Serum chromogranin A and gastric biopsies should assess for enterochromaffin-like cell hyperplasia or type 1 gastric neuroendocrine tumours, which develop in 1-5% of patients with long-standing autoimmune achlorhydria. Serum pepsinogen I and the pepsinogen I/II ratio are validated non-invasive biomarkers of corpus atrophy, used increasingly in surveillance protocols in high-prevalence regions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies autoimmune atrophic gastritis as the cause; anti-parietal-cell antibodies are sensitive (90%) but non-specific; anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies are highly specific for pernicious anaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results typically available within a few days. A positive result indicates the need for endoscopy and B12 testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A markedly elevated gastrin (often above 1,000 pg/mL) in the absence of a proton pump inhibitor strongly suggests achlorhydria because low acid removes the feedback brake on gastrin release.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting morning blood draw; PPIs must be withheld for one to two weeks before the test for accurate interpretation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the gastric mucosa and provides tissue for histological staging of atrophy (OLGA) and metaplasia (OLGIM), defining cancer risk and surveillance interval.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible camera is passed under sedation through the mouth into the stomach; multiple pinch biopsies from the body and antrum are painless. Results take five to seven days.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum pepsinogen I and pepsinogen I/II ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: A low pepsinogen I (below 70 ng/mL) and a low ratio (below 3) indicate corpus atrophy, serving as a blood-based surrogate for the endoscopic biopsy finding.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; a cost-effective first-line screening test before endoscopy in high-risk individuals or high-prevalence settings.

diagnosticTests.test: H. pylori testing (urea breath test or stool antigen)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies active infection so that eradication therapy can be offered to halt progression of environmental atrophic gastritis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urea breath test: you drink a small amount of urea solution, then breathe into a bag after 30 minutes. Stool antigen: a small stool sample is sent to the laboratory. Both are accurate and non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, B12, folate, and serum iron studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the nutritional consequences of achlorhydria — iron-deficiency anaemia, megaloblastic anaemia, and B12 deficiency — to guide supplementation and treatment urgency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of achlorhydria targets three goals: correcting the underlying cause where possible, replacing deficient micronutrients, and monitoring for complications including gastric neoplasia. For H. pylori-related atrophic gastritis, eradication is the only proven intervention that halts progression; standard triple or quadruple antibiotic therapy achieves eradication in 85-95% of cases and can lead to partial mucosal recovery over years in patients without advanced atrophy. Autoimmune atrophic gastritis has no disease-modifying treatment; management centres on lifelong B12 replacement — preferably by intramuscular injection (hydroxocobalamin 1 mg every three months) to bypass the absent intrinsic factor, though high-dose oral B12 (1,000 mcg daily) is an effective alternative in patients who cannot tolerate injections. Iron deficiency is corrected with oral ferrous sulphate or, when absorption is severely impaired, intravenous iron. PPI-induced achlorhydria is fully reversed by stopping the drug at the lowest clinically justified dose, followed by review of whether ongoing acid suppression is actually needed. Calcium absorption is reduced in the absence of acid, and vitamin D and calcium supplementation reduce the long-term bone-density consequences of profound acid suppression. Endoscopic surveillance is recommended according to the severity of atrophy and the presence of intestinal metaplasia; European guidelines (MAPS II, 2019) stratify patients into low, intermediate, and high risk, with high-risk patients (extensive atrophy and metaplasia or incomplete intestinal metaplasia type III) surveyed every one to two years by endoscopy with systematic biopsies.

medicalTreatments.name: H. pylori eradication therapy
medicalTreatments.description: For environmental atrophic gastritis caused by H. pylori, standard triple therapy (proton pump inhibitor, clarithromycin, amoxicillin for 14 days) or bismuth quadruple therapy achieves eradication and halts the inflammatory drive toward further atrophy. Post-eradication surveillance confirms success with urea breath test or stool antigen four or more weeks after completing antibiotics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradication with modern regimens achieved in 85-95% of patients; mucosal recovery is documented histologically in up to 50% of patients without advanced metaplasia at five-year follow-up.

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin B12 replacement
medicalTreatments.description: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin (1 mg every three months after loading doses) is the standard for autoimmune achlorhydria with established B12 deficiency. High-dose oral B12 (1,000 mcg daily) exploits passive diffusion and is effective for patients who cannot tolerate injections, including those on anticoagulants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Neurological symptoms improve in approximately 60-80% of patients if treatment is started before irreversible spinal cord damage; haematological normalisation occurs in most patients within 8-12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Oral ferrous sulphate (200 mg twice daily between meals) corrects iron-deficiency anaemia in mild to moderate cases. Intravenous iron sucrose or ferric carboxymaltose achieves more reliable repletion when oral absorption is impaired by achlorhydria or when the patient cannot tolerate oral iron.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV iron restores haemoglobin in 85-95% of patients within 4-8 weeks; oral iron is effective in mild cases but absorption is approximately 30-50% lower in achlorhydric patients than in those with normal acid.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium carbonate requires an acid environment for adequate absorption; patients with achlorhydria should use calcium citrate instead, which is acid-independent. Combined with vitamin D 800-2,000 IU daily, this maintains bone density and reduces the long-term fracture risk associated with acid suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Calcium citrate maintains bone mineral density significantly better than calcium carbonate in achlorhydric patients; vitamin D supplementation reduces fracture risk by approximately 20-30% in deficient older adults.

medicalTreatments.name: PPI dose reduction or cessation
medicalTreatments.description: When achlorhydria is drug-induced, careful review of the PPI indication with the prescribing physician is essential. Many patients on long-term PPIs no longer have an active indication; stepping down to the lowest effective dose or to an H2 receptor antagonist often resolves profound acid suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intragastric pH normalises within one to two weeks of PPI cessation in all patients; risk of B12 and iron deficiency decreases proportionally after drug withdrawal.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary acid-enhancing strategies
alternativeTreatments.description: Eating foods naturally rich in organic acids — such as apple cider vinegar in water, citrus juices, or fermented foods — has a weak evidence base for augmenting gastric acidity, but some patients find symptom relief. These are adjuncts and do not replace micronutrient supplementation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Betaine hydrochloride supplements
alternativeTreatments.description: Betaine HCl capsules taken with meals are used by some clinicians as a temporary acidifying supplement in hypochlorhydria, particularly to improve protein digestion and iron absorption. Evidence from randomised trials is limited; it should not be taken by patients with active gastric or oesophageal disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium probiotics may partly suppress bacterial overgrowth associated with achlorhydria. Evidence for clinical benefit in achlorhydria specifically is preliminary, but probiotics have a good safety profile and may reduce diarrhoea in susceptible patients.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist should be involved whenever achlorhydria is suspected, because the required investigations — endoscopy with systematic biopsies for OLGA staging and H. pylori testing — are specialist procedures. Ongoing surveillance for gastric atrophy and intestinal metaplasia, given the cancer risk, requires a specialist-led protocol. For the haematological and neurological consequences of B12 deficiency, coordination with a haematologist or neurologist may be needed in advanced cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a gastroenterologist with an interest in pre-malignant gastric conditions and endoscopic surveillance. Experience with OLGA and OLGIM staging and familiarity with MAPS II European guidelines is valuable. Where autoimmune atrophic gastritis co-exists with other autoimmune conditions, a multidisciplinary approach involving an endocrinologist or rheumatologist adds value. Confirm the practice follows a structured surveillance protocol rather than offering one-off endoscopy.
preventionStrategies: Test for and eradicate H. pylori whenever it is found, particularly in first-degree relatives of patients with gastric cancer or atrophic gastritis.
Use proton pump inhibitors only at the lowest effective dose and for the shortest necessary duration; have the indication reviewed annually.
Ensure adequate dietary intake of vitamin B12 (primarily from meat, fish, eggs, and dairy) and iron to reduce the clinical impact if achlorhydria develops.
Screen family members of patients with autoimmune atrophic gastritis or pernicious anaemia for anti-parietal-cell antibodies, as the condition is familial.
Follow recommended endoscopic surveillance intervals once atrophic gastritis is diagnosed, so pre-cancerous changes can be detected and treated early.
lifestyleModifications: Eat smaller, more frequent meals; a smaller bolus of food places less demand on a stomach with reduced acid and enzyme capacity.
Chew food thoroughly to begin mechanical breakdown before it reaches the stomach.
Avoid high-dose PPI therapy unless clinically mandatory, and discuss step-down therapy with your prescribing clinician.
Choose calcium citrate rather than calcium carbonate supplements, since the citrate form does not require acid for absorption.
Limit alcohol and NSAIDs, which can worsen mucosal inflammation in an already atrophic stomach.
Maintain an adequate intake of vitamin C, which may modestly improve non-haem iron absorption in a low-acid environment.

recommended: Vitamin B12-rich foods: red meat, fish, shellfish, eggs, and dairy products
Haem iron sources: red meat, liver, and dark poultry — more readily absorbed than plant-based iron
Vitamin C-rich foods alongside non-haem iron sources to maximise absorption
Fermented foods such as yoghurt, kefir, and kimchi to support gut microbiome diversity and reduce bacterial overgrowth
Well-cooked, soft proteins that are easier to partially digest with reduced acid and pepsin activity
avoid: Large meals that overwhelm the reduced digestive capacity of an achlorhydric stomach
High-dose calcium carbonate supplements, which are poorly absorbed without acid
Excessive dietary fibre immediately at mealtimes, which can bind iron and reduce its absorption
Raw shellfish and undercooked meat, which pose a higher infection risk when the acid sterilisation barrier is absent
Excessive alcohol, which damages the already compromised gastric mucosa

exerciseGuidelines: There are no exercise restrictions specific to achlorhydria in patients who have corrected their nutritional deficiencies. Anaemia from iron or B12 deficiency significantly limits exercise tolerance; exertional symptoms should improve within two to three months of appropriate supplementation. Until anaemia resolves, low-intensity activity such as walking is recommended over high-intensity exercise. Once nutritional status is normal, full exercise participation is encouraged.
dailyManagement: Take B12 injections on schedule; do not extend the interval between doses once established on a maintenance regimen.
Take iron supplements on an empty stomach with a small amount of vitamin C-rich juice to maximise absorption; separate from calcium supplements by at least two hours.
Attend all scheduled endoscopic surveillance appointments without delay, as atrophy and metaplasia can progress silently.
Monitor for symptoms of B12 deficiency — numbness, tingling, unsteady walking, or memory problems — and report them promptly, as neurological damage is more reversible when treated early.
Keep a list of all medications that suppress acid output and discuss alternatives with your prescribing clinician.
prognosis: The prognosis of achlorhydria depends primarily on the underlying cause and how promptly its nutritional consequences are identified and treated. Drug-induced achlorhydria carries an excellent prognosis: all biochemical and nutritional effects reverse on stopping or reducing the causative medication. For autoimmune atrophic gastritis, the condition is irreversible but its complications — B12 deficiency, iron deficiency, and anaemia — are fully correctable with appropriate supplementation, and most patients maintain excellent quality of life with lifelong B12 injections. The main long-term risk is gastric neoplasia: the absolute lifetime risk of gastric cancer in autoimmune atrophic gastritis is approximately 1-3%, and the risk of type 1 gastric neuroendocrine tumours is 1-5%; both can be detected early through scheduled endoscopic surveillance. H. pylori-related atrophic gastritis with successful eradication carries a lower cancer risk than untreated active infection, with one meta-analysis estimating a 54% reduction in gastric cancer incidence after eradication in high-risk populations.
recoveryTimeline: Haematological recovery from B12 deficiency, measured by normalisation of blood count and serum B12, occurs within eight to twelve weeks of starting replacement therapy. Iron-deficiency anaemia corrects within three to six months with adequate supplementation. Neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency improve over months if treatment begins promptly, but can take up to two years to fully resolve and may be permanent when damage is severe. Drug-induced achlorhydria biochemically reverses within one to two weeks of stopping the causative PPI.
complications: Vitamin B12 deficiency leading to megaloblastic anaemia and, if untreated, subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — the most serious complication and largely preventable with early treatment.
Iron-deficiency anaemia from impaired conversion and absorption of dietary iron, causing fatigue, exercise intolerance, and reduced cognitive function.
Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth from loss of the gastric acid bacterial barrier, producing chronic diarrhoea, malabsorption, and nutritional deficiencies.
Increased susceptibility to enteric infections including Salmonella, Campylobacter, Clostridioides difficile, and cholera, because infective dose requirements are much lower in the absence of acid.
Gastric neoplasia: type 1 gastric neuroendocrine tumour and, less commonly, gastric adenocarcinoma, arising from chronic hypergastrinaemia and intestinal metaplasia in autoimmune atrophic gastritis.

Reduced calcium absorption and osteoporosis, particularly with long-term PPI-induced achlorhydria, increasing fracture risk by approximately 20-40% compared with non-users in some observational studies.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Gastritis
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly overview of gastric conditions including atrophic gastritis from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pernicious anaemia and B12 deficiency
supportResources.description: UK guidance on the diagnosis and management of B12 deficiency, including information on pernicious anaemia linked to autoimmune achlorhydria.

supportResources.name: Pernicious Anaemia Society
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and support organisation providing resources for people with pernicious anaemia and autoimmune atrophic gastritis.

sources.title: Annibale B et al. Clinical features of type A chronic atrophic gastritis. World Journal of Gastroenterology, 2014
Dinis-Ribeiro M et al. MAPS II: Management of Epithelial Precancerous Conditions and Lesions in the Stomach (2019 update). Endoscopy, 2019
Howden CW, Hunt RH. Guidelines for the Management of Helicobacter pylori Infection. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 1998
Lam JR et al. Proton Pump Inhibitor and Histamine-2 Receptor Antagonist Use and Vitamin B12 Deficiency. JAMA, 2013
Ford AC et al. Helicobacter pylori Eradication Therapy to Prevent Gastric Cancer in Healthy Asymptomatic Infected Individuals. BMJ, 2020
Yang YX et al. Long-term Proton Pump Inhibitor Therapy and Risk of Hip Fracture. JAMA, 2006

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroparesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gastroparesis causes slow stomach emptying with normal or elevated acid; achlorhydria causes absent acid with normal or even accelerated emptying. Both produce nausea and bloating but are distinguished by pH testing and emptying scintigraphy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Functional dyspepsia has normal acid output and no structural abnormality; achlorhydria has measurably absent acid and specific antibody markers, and is confirmed by endoscopy and biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD reflects excess acid entering the oesophagus; achlorhydria involves absent acid in the stomach. The symptoms of bloating and discomfort overlap, but pH testing distinguishes them clearly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron-deficiency anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achlorhydria is an important but often missed underlying cause of iron-deficiency anaemia; if anaemia recurs despite iron replacement, testing for achlorhydria and malabsorption should be performed.

relatedConditions.name: Vitamin B12 deficiency anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Achlorhydria in autoimmune atrophic gastritis is the leading cause of vitamin B12 malabsorption in adults because both acid and intrinsic factor are lost together.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroparesis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both reflect impaired gastric physiology; autoimmune or post-surgical achlorhydria can co-occur with gastroparesis when extensive gastric damage affects both secretory and motor function.

relatedConditions.name: Coeliac disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Coeliac disease is associated with a higher prevalence of autoimmune atrophic gastritis, and both conditions together produce compounded micronutrient deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic atrophic gastritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic atrophic gastritis is the histological substrate of achlorhydria; the two diagnoses represent the same disease process at a pathological and clinical level.

faqs.question: What is achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Achlorhydria means the stomach produces no hydrochloric acid at all. Normal fasting stomach acid has a pH of 1.5 to 3.5; in achlorhydria it stays above 7. This impairs protein digestion, reduces absorption of vitamin B12 and iron, and removes the acid barrier that kills ingested bacteria.

faqs.question: What causes achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: The most common causes today are long-term proton pump inhibitor use (which reversibly suppresses acid), autoimmune destruction of the stomach's acid-secreting cells (autoimmune atrophic gastritis causing pernicious anaemia), and chronic H. pylori infection that progressively scars the stomach lining. Gastric surgery that removes stomach tissue is also a cause.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Bloating, nausea, and early fullness after meals are the most common symptoms. Fatigue and pallor from iron-deficiency anaemia, and numbness or tingling in the hands and feet from vitamin B12 deficiency, are characteristic downstream effects. Many people have no clear symptoms until a blood test reveals anaemia or B12 deficiency.

faqs.question: How is achlorhydria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Elevated fasting serum gastrin and low serum pepsinogen I suggest the diagnosis. Anti-parietal-cell and anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies confirm autoimmune atrophic gastritis. Endoscopy with biopsies from the stomach body provides definitive histological staging of atrophy. B12, iron, and blood count tests quantify the nutritional impact.

faqs.question: Can achlorhydria be cured?
faqs.answer: Drug-induced achlorhydria fully reverses when the proton pump inhibitor is stopped. H. pylori-related atrophic gastritis halts progression after eradication, with some mucosal recovery in early cases. Autoimmune atrophic gastritis cannot be reversed, but its consequences — B12 and iron deficiency — are fully correctable with lifelong supplementation.

faqs.question: Does achlorhydria cause vitamin B12 deficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in autoimmune atrophic gastritis. Vitamin B12 in food is bound to protein; stomach acid is needed to separate it so intrinsic factor can attach. In autoimmune achlorhydria, both acid and intrinsic factor are absent, blocking B12 absorption entirely. Without treatment, B12 deficiency causes anaemia and irreversible neurological damage.

faqs.question: What is the difference between achlorhydria and hypochlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Hypochlorhydria means reduced acid production; achlorhydria means complete absence. Both sit on the same spectrum. Hypochlorhydria is much more common, especially in older adults and PPI users, while true achlorhydria requires an objective test — stimulation testing showing pH above 7 — to confirm.

faqs.question: Can proton pump inhibitors cause achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Long-term daily PPI use can suppress acid to achlorhydric levels in some patients, raising gastric pH to 5 to 7. This is fully reversible on stopping the drug. However, it can cause B12 deficiency after years of use, and patients on long-term PPIs should have periodic blood counts and B12 levels checked.

faqs.question: Is achlorhydria dangerous?
faqs.answer: Achlorhydria itself is not immediately life-threatening but its consequences are serious if missed. Untreated B12 deficiency damages the spinal cord in a way that may become permanent. Persistent iron-deficiency anaemia impairs cognitive and physical function. Autoimmune achlorhydria also carries a 1-3% lifetime risk of gastric cancer, managed by endoscopic surveillance.

faqs.question: Does achlorhydria increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Autoimmune atrophic gastritis with achlorhydria is associated with a modestly elevated lifetime risk of gastric adenocarcinoma (approximately 1-3%) and type 1 gastric neuroendocrine tumours (1-5%). Regular endoscopic surveillance with biopsies every one to three years, depending on risk stage, allows early detection when treatment is curative.

faqs.question: What diet helps with achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Focus on B12-rich foods (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) and haem-iron sources (red meat, dark poultry). Eat smaller meals and chew thoroughly to aid mechanical digestion. Vitamin C with plant-based iron meals improves absorption in low-acid conditions. Fermented foods may help limit bacterial overgrowth. Supplement with calcium citrate rather than carbonate, which requires acid for absorption.

faqs.question: How does achlorhydria affect iron absorption?
faqs.answer: Gastric acid converts dietary ferric iron (Fe³+) to the ferrous form (Fe²+) that the intestine can absorb. Without acid, this conversion is impaired, reducing iron absorption significantly even when dietary intake is adequate. This explains why iron-deficiency anaemia recurs in achlorhydric patients despite oral iron therapy — intravenous iron often achieves more reliable correction.

faqs.question: What is autoimmune atrophic gastritis?
faqs.answer: Autoimmune atrophic gastritis is an immune-mediated condition in which the body's own antibodies attack and destroy the parietal cells in the stomach body that produce acid and intrinsic factor. Over years this produces achlorhydria and pernicious anaemia. It is associated with other autoimmune diseases including Hashimoto thyroiditis and is more common in women and people of Northern European descent.

faqs.question: Can H. pylori cause achlorhydria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic H. pylori infection causes progressive atrophy of the acid-secreting gastric mucosa over decades in a minority of infected individuals. Unlike autoimmune achlorhydria, the H. pylori form can be halted by eradicating the bacteria, and early partial recovery of acid glands is possible in younger patients before irreversible scarring sets in.

faqs.question: Does achlorhydria cause bacterial overgrowth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gastric acid normally kills most ingested bacteria before they reach the small intestine. When acid is absent, bacteria from the mouth and upper gut survive and can overpopulate the small intestine, causing small intestinal bacterial overgrowth. This produces bloating, diarrhoea, and worsens nutrient malabsorption on top of the direct absorptive defects from achlorhydria.

faqs.question: How is B12 deficiency from achlorhydria treated?
faqs.answer: Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin (1 mg every three months after loading doses) bypasses the absorptive defect caused by absent intrinsic factor. High-dose oral B12 (1,000 mcg daily) works through passive absorption and is effective for patients who cannot tolerate injections. Treatment must continue for life in autoimmune achlorhydria because the underlying condition does not resolve.

faqs.question: Can achlorhydria be prevented?
faqs.answer: H. pylori-related achlorhydria is preventable by testing for and eradicating H. pylori, especially in people with a family history of gastric cancer or atrophic gastritis. Drug-induced achlorhydria is prevented by using PPIs only when there is a clear medical indication, at the lowest effective dose. Autoimmune atrophic gastritis has no known prevention strategy.

faqs.question: What supplements do people with achlorhydria need?
faqs.answer: Vitamin B12 injections or high-dose oral B12 are essential for autoimmune achlorhydria. Iron supplementation — preferably intravenous for confirmed malabsorption — corrects iron-deficiency anaemia. Calcium citrate (not carbonate) and vitamin D maintain bone density. A gastroenterologist or dietitian should guide supplementation based on blood test results.

faqs.question: How often should people with achlorhydria have an endoscopy?
faqs.answer: Surveillance frequency depends on the histological severity of atrophy and intestinal metaplasia, staged using the OLGA or OLGIM system at first endoscopy. Low-risk patients (mild, focal atrophy) may need no routine surveillance. High-risk patients — those with extensive atrophy, extensive intestinal metaplasia, or type III intestinal metaplasia — should have endoscopy with biopsies every one to two years.

faqs.question: Is achlorhydria related to pernicious anaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pernicious anaemia is the haematological consequence of autoimmune achlorhydria. When autoimmune destruction eliminates both acid-secreting parietal cells and intrinsic factor, vitamin B12 cannot be absorbed, and over years red blood cell production falters, producing macrocytic anaemia. Treating pernicious anaemia with B12 injections corrects the anaemia but not the underlying achlorhydria.

metaDescription: Achlorhydria means no stomach acid, causing B12 deficiency, iron anaemia and infection risk. Learn causes, diagnosis and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
